title: Youth culture of the 1960s
description: Test your skills on the generational clashes of the 1960s!

assessment_items.question_content:
> “But in recent years, the withdrawal from the hysteria of the Cold War and the development of a more aggressive, activist foreign policy have done much to force many of us to rethink attitudes that were deep and basic sentiments about our country. And now the incredible war in Vietnam has provided the razor, the terrifying sharp cutting edge that has finally severed the last vestiges of our illusion that morality and democracy are the guiding principles of American foreign policy. . . .

>“Not even the President can say that we are defending freedom in Vietnam. Perhaps what the President wants to say is that we are attempting to defend the freedom of the American people.” 

>-Source: Paul Potter, president of Students for a Democratic Society (SDS), “The Incredible War,” 1965

**People who shared Potter’s ideas at the time most typically sought to achieve their goals by doing which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: hosting teach-ins on college campuses to debate American involvement in the war
filing legal challenges against the United States government for exceeding its constitutional powers
using violent demonstrations like bombing federal buildings to bring the threat of war home
boycotting American-made products to hurt the US economy and end the war

assessment_items.question_content:
> “But in recent years, the withdrawal from the hysteria of the Cold War and the development of a more aggressive, activist foreign policy have done much to force many of us to rethink attitudes that were deep and basic sentiments about our country. And now the incredible war in Vietnam has provided the razor, the terrifying sharp cutting edge that has finally severed the last vestiges of our illusion that morality and democracy are the guiding principles of American foreign policy. . . .

>“Not even the President can say that we are defending freedom in Vietnam. Perhaps what the President wants to say is that we are attempting to defend the freedom of the American people.” 

>-Source: Paul Potter, president of Students for a Democratic Society (SDS), “The Incredible War,” 1965

**Which of the following aspects of the author’s description expresses a major change in Americans’ views of the Cold War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the idea that the United States should not intervene in the affairs of other countries
the idea that the United States intervened in Vietnam for its own financial interests
the idea that the United States only helped countries to access their natural resources
the idea that the United States should stop the spread of communism into vulnerable countries

assessment_items.question_content:
> “But in recent years, the withdrawal from the hysteria of the Cold War and the development of a more aggressive, activist foreign policy have done much to force many of us to rethink attitudes that were deep and basic sentiments about our country. And now the incredible war in Vietnam has provided the razor, the terrifying sharp cutting edge that has finally severed the last vestiges of our illusion that morality and democracy are the guiding principles of American foreign policy. . . .

>“Not even the President can say that we are defending freedom in Vietnam. Perhaps what the President wants to say is that we are attempting to defend the freedom of the American people.” 

>-Source: Paul Potter, president of Students for a Democratic Society (SDS), “The Incredible War,” 1965

**The excerpt best serves as evidence of which of the following trends during the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of an antiwar movement that protested the Vietnam War
the rise in support for an Equal Rights Amendment for women
the efforts of members of Congress to limit the president’s war powers
the impact of the amendment that lowered the minimum voting age to eighteen

assessment_items.question_content:
> “We saw the seriousness of the early antiwar movement not so much in an analysis of whether it was a minority or a majority— it was always a minority. We saw that as affecting overall public opinion, we saw that as affecting the Congress, and as furthering the defection of the press. . . . This might be a bunch of wild-eyed kids or little old ladies in tennis shoes walking down Pennsylvania Avenue. . . but it had an insidious effect in public opinion and in the Congress.” 

> -Source: Roger Morris, in an interview with Tom Wells, printed in *The War Within: America’s Battle Over Vietnam,* 1994

**Which of the following pieces of evidence could best be used to support the argument in the excerpt about how the antiwar movement affected Congressional actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the introduction of the War Powers Act of 1973
the calls to impeach President Richard Nixon
the development of the credibility gap
the emergence of a distinct youth culture

assessment_items.question_content:
> “We saw the seriousness of the early antiwar movement not so much in an analysis of whether it was a minority or a majority— it was always a minority. We saw that as affecting overall public opinion, we saw that as affecting the Congress, and as furthering the defection of the press. . . . This might be a bunch of wild-eyed kids or little old ladies in tennis shoes walking down Pennsylvania Avenue. . . but it had an insidious effect in public opinion and in the Congress.” 

> -Source: Roger Morris, in an interview with Tom Wells, printed in *The War Within: America’s Battle Over Vietnam,* 1994

**According to the passage, which of the following was the most important effect that the antiwar movement had on society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It swayed the American public towards disapproval of the war.
It led to the American electorate replacing Johnson with Nixon.
It divided American voters along generational lines.
It caused the American public not to trust newspaper reports.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “We saw the seriousness of the early antiwar movement not so much in an analysis of whether it was a minority or a majority— it was always a minority. We saw that as affecting overall public opinion, we saw that as affecting the Congress, and as furthering the defection of the press. . . . This might be a bunch of wild-eyed kids or little old ladies in tennis shoes walking down Pennsylvania Avenue. . . but it had an insidious effect in public opinion and in the Congress.” 

> -Source: Roger Morris, in an interview with Tom Wells, printed in *The War Within: America’s Battle Over Vietnam,* 1994

**By the 1970s, the ideas of the antiwar movement contributed most directly to the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: withdrawal of American troops from Vietnam.
ratification of the Twenty-fourth Amendment.
negotiation of a peace treaty between North and South Vietnam.
impeachment of Richard Nixon after he authorized the bombing of Cambodia.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There is at the outset a very obvious and almost facile connection between the war in Vietnam and the struggle I and others have been waging in America. A few years ago there was a shining moment in that struggle. It seemed as if there was a real promise of hope for the poor, both black and white, through the poverty program. There were experiments, hopes, new beginnings. Then came the buildup in Vietnam, and I watched this program broken and eviscerated as if it were some idle political plaything of a society gone mad on war. And I knew that America would never invest the necessary funds or energies in rehabilitation of its poor so long as adventures like Vietnam continued to draw men and skills and money like some demonic, destructive suction tube.”

>-Source: Martin Luther King, Jr., “Beyond Vietnam,” 1967

**The excerpt reflects some antiwar protestors’ belief at the time that the federal government was:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: not doing enough to address domestic social and economic issues.
expanding the president’s power beyond what the Constitution allowed.
transitioning towards a conservative ideology after implementing the Great Society.
not offering enough support to underdeveloped countries fighting communism.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There is at the outset a very obvious and almost facile connection between the war in Vietnam and the struggle I and others have been waging in America. A few years ago there was a shining moment in that struggle. It seemed as if there was a real promise of hope for the poor, both black and white, through the poverty program. There were experiments, hopes, new beginnings. Then came the buildup in Vietnam, and I watched this program broken and eviscerated as if it were some idle political plaything of a society gone mad on war. And I knew that America would never invest the necessary funds or energies in rehabilitation of its poor so long as adventures like Vietnam continued to draw men and skills and money like some demonic, destructive suction tube.”

>-Source: Martin Luther King, Jr., “Beyond Vietnam,” 1967

**Which of the following aspects of the author’s description expresses a major change in Americans’ views of the Vietnam War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the idea that the United States pursued immoral policies abroad instead of addressing domestic issues
the idea that the United States has a responsibility for protecting democratic institutions across the world
the idea that the United States’ involvement in the Vietnam War led to a rise in unemployment
the idea that the American commitment to civil rights only strengthened as the Vietnam War continued

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There is at the outset a very obvious and almost facile connection between the war in Vietnam and the struggle I and others have been waging in America. A few years ago there was a shining moment in that struggle. It seemed as if there was a real promise of hope for the poor, both black and white, through the poverty program. There were experiments, hopes, new beginnings. Then came the buildup in Vietnam, and I watched this program broken and eviscerated as if it were some idle political plaything of a society gone mad on war. And I knew that America would never invest the necessary funds or energies in rehabilitation of its poor so long as adventures like Vietnam continued to draw men and skills and money like some demonic, destructive suction tube.”

>-Source: Martin Luther King, Jr., “Beyond Vietnam,” 1967

**The ideas expressed in the passage most strongly influenced which United States foreign policy decision in the 1970s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the efforts to train Southern Vietnamese soldiers and withdraw American soldiers
the reallocation of the federal budget to increase military spending and reduce infrastructure spending
the creation of an international collective security organization for mutual defense
the negotiations to end the international conflict with the Soviet Union in a series of peace talks

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The Pill! The fact that it is referred to so majestically represents something of the measure of importance that is generally attached to this genuinely revolutionary development. For it is a revolutionary development, probably to be ranked among the half dozen or so major innovations in man’s two or more million years of history. In its effects I believe that the pill ranks in importance with the discovery of fire, the creation and employment of tools, the development of hunting, the invention of agriculture, the development of urbanism, scientific medicine, and the release and control of nuclear energy.” 

>-Source: Ashley Montagu, philosopher, *Sex, Man and Society,* 1969

**The excerpt provided is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of a sexual revolution
the Supreme Court decision in *Roe v. Wade*
the rise of the Moral Majority
the development of a baby boom

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The Pill! The fact that it is referred to so majestically represents something of the measure of importance that is generally attached to this genuinely revolutionary development. For it is a revolutionary development, probably to be ranked among the half dozen or so major innovations in man’s two or more million years of history. In its effects I believe that the pill ranks in importance with the discovery of fire, the creation and employment of tools, the development of hunting, the invention of agriculture, the development of urbanism, scientific medicine, and the release and control of nuclear energy.” 

>-Source: Ashley Montagu, philosopher, *Sex, Man and Society,* 1969

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how new inventions challenged traditional sexual norms
how the birth control pill transformed gender roles for men
how the sexual revolution led to a conservative backlash
how the creation of the birth control pill ended the sexual revolution

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The Pill! The fact that it is referred to so majestically represents something of the measure of importance that is generally attached to this genuinely revolutionary development. For it is a revolutionary development, probably to be ranked among the half dozen or so major innovations in man’s two or more million years of history. In its effects I believe that the pill ranks in importance with the discovery of fire, the creation and employment of tools, the development of hunting, the invention of agriculture, the development of urbanism, scientific medicine, and the release and control of nuclear energy.” 

>-Source: Ashley Montagu, philosopher, *Sex, Man and Society,* 1969

**The excerpt most directly reflects which of the following trends of the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the changes in sexual attitudes and sexual behaviors
the influence of liberal political ideas on cultural beliefs
the increasing public belief in workplace gender equality
the growth in political power for the younger generation
